speaker: interviewer
text: It's July 17, 1995. Interviewing Jack Skovronski. Tape number two. When we left off, you were talking about--

speaker: interviewee
text: About the city of Krasnovodsk

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: So that-- this is a port on the Caspian Sea, and borders with Persia, or Iran. So in a few days-- I don't exactly remember the days, how much we were on the-- on there, but it wasn't too long. About four or five days, I think, it took us to get to Iran. Once we got to Iran, there were the Jewish agency in Iran, very active in helping Jewish boys which came through that-- on that ship. So I even went-- was invited for a Shabbos dinner. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] he promised that he's going to see that we get some kind of a work.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we were about four, five boys my age, a little older. And we got his address. We know where he lives, so we came. And he gave us a job as painters.

speaker: interviewee
text: We painted a hotel, which I can't remember the name anymore. But that was under the Shah. And the Jews had a wonderful time in Iran before Khomeini came to power.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we had a wonderful time. We stayed there for a little while. And it must have been not more-- because this was February, and in March, end of March, I was already in Israel. So the whole thing lasted maybe three, four weeks.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they packed us on trucks. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Of course, they didn't accept us in the Polish army, but we were going as civilians with the army in the same trucks. And we went through Iraq, from Iran to Iraq, from Iraq to Transjordan, and from Transjordan to Palestine. While being in Iran, the Jewish agency in Palestine was fighting, because not many Jews came from Europe at all because there was the war going on. So the British government gave a permit for, like, all of us that were about-- all of us were 120 Jews. They gave certificates, permits, to enter Palestine legally. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So we came with the trucks to Palestine. The Jewish agency took us back to the-- to a kibbutz which is on the other side of Jordan, and told us to-- not to cross the border illegally, but wake up the-- the guys that stay on the border, which were British soldiers, and show them the certificates that we have. So they stamped it, and we were legally in Palestine. Legally in Palestine.

speaker: interviewee
text: After a while being in Palestine, we were in contact with the Polish embassy in-- in Tel Aviv. And they were trying to find all kinds of jobs for refugees. So among other was a job in Eritrea, which is next to Abyssinia. It was under Italian rule. And that was the colony freed from the Italians. In 1942, they were already free.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we went there to-- to work in the British-- under the British mandate. And we were there with a group of Polish Christians and a group of Jews. Of course, we couldn't get along too well with them, and after a while went back to Palestine. We were there, also, about 12 months, a year, or a little over a year.

speaker: interviewee
text: We went to Egypt. We were in British uniforms. And we went to Sudan. From Sudan to Eritrea, from Eritrea back to Sudan, to Cairo, and from Cairo, we went back to Palestine. And that was in 1944. 1944, we-- I took a job in Israel, all kind of job, whatever it was available. Manual jobs. And I worked for a couple of years. And in 1947, I met my wife-- my future wife. And we got married, and we are married for 48 years till now. We have two wonderful children, which are very proud of. And a new life began in Israel. We got married. We had to support a family, so I did all kind of-- all kind of work. Delivered bread to the-- to the stores from a bakery. And I became a member of a-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] a cooperative that was organized. They were, like, self-- self-supporting, self-sufficient. And we had it not bad. And one of my brothers, Henry-- which survived the Holocaust-- came from Germany to the United States. And he-- I was in touch with him, and after letters and conversations, we decided to come to the United States.

speaker: interviewer
text: So in 1957-- it took a long time, because during the war it was very hard to get a permit to come to the United States. But finally we got it. In 1957, I left Israel.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the mean time, two of our girls were born there. And we came to-- in 1957, Jona was 10 years old. Nicki was six years old. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And we started again a new life, all over again. Times weren't easy. It was hard, just as good as in any other country. But, you see, right now, thank God, we worked hard and we accomplished a little, whatever we accomplished. We have a nice home. We've got wonderful kids, wonderful grandchildren. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Very happy. I'm 75 now, and [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] retired and looking for a-- just to keep on going, as far as we can.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, if you have any other more questions? OK. Well, I would-- I'd like to go back a little.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: And maybe get a little more--

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: Some details.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let's go-- go back to when the war started.

speaker: interviewee
text: Uh-huh.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was it like in-- in your town when-- what-- what-- what incidents happened? How did you know that things were going to get worse?

speaker: interviewee
text: Worse. Of course, they were-- besides the forced labor, they did a lot of things. They burned our synagogue. And they said that the Jews did it, and we had to pay ransom for it. So everybody that had gold or silver or money, whatever we could, everybody brought, generously, to the-- to the committee, Jewish committee, that they formed, and we paid them a ransom for that synagogue burned down. And they had to sign the-- a few people. I don't know if my father was among them. I don't remember, exactly. But they signed that they did-- they burned down the synagogue.

speaker: interviewer
text: So these things doesn't do you any good. So you-- you-- you try to make decisions in your life. So that was the decision that pushed me out of the-- out of the home town, like I mentioned before. Were your-- were your brothers or your sisters-- were others in your family having to go to the labor-- do labor, or?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, most-- my-- my brother, the younger one, was in the Polish army. And the war-- the war was ended after a few weeks, all over. So he came home, and how he got-- I wasn't home at-- no more. And the correspondence was very poor between Russia and Germany, so I didn't know. But I-- like, I later found out my brother end up in-- both of them. Both brothers were in concentration camps. My sister went with my parents to Chelmno. My older sister, with her husband and child, I don't even know how they perished, because they were also living in a little close-by town called Chodecz. And I don't know exactly how they perished. But I do know that my sister and my younger brother went to the Chelmno, to the camp to [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: And we-- How did-- how did you find out what happened to your parents and your--

speaker: interviewee
text: After the war. Nothing during the war. There was no-- no-- no correspondence, because when we were in Palestine in 1942, '43, there were not much coming news out of-- of course, we knew the general news that they are-- what they're doing to the Jews. But we didn't know exactly what, individually, they did to my family. But I know that my older brother survived in Germany. And after the liberation, he died after the liberation. [INAUDIBLE]. My younger brother survived also through-- told me, one day, a story that they took him in the ovens, in the crematorium, and everybody between them, they said, if you open your shoelaces and you open all your this-- shirt, and-- the gas will kill you faster. At that moment, a sound was [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] coming. And-- and they said that the Red Cross came to visit that camp. So they took everybody out of the crematorium. And that was my brother, how he survived.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you made the decision to leave, leave Przedecz, were you-- was there friends of yours that you all went together?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. We went about three-- two brothers, and one girl and I-- four of us. We went to Warsaw. From Warsaw to Siemiatycze, a town by the River Bug, which was the dividing line between Russia and-- Germany and Russia, occupied territories. So--

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you travel? How did you get from Przedecz to--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, to Warsaw wasn't too bad to travel, because trains were still going. And we went with a train. And from Warsaw to Siemiatycze, [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] horses, buggies, and all kind of-- I don't exactly remember how far it was, but we came at night. And the borders were watched by-- on one side by the Germans, on one side by the Russians. And we went through with a little boat, a row boat, to the other side. And just coincidentally, there was nobody by the border. But once we crossed there, they didn't send us back. They caught us, and they let us go. So that's how I wind up in the City of Rowne with my landsmen, my hometown people.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have to-- I mean, did you have to bribe anyone or get false papers?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah. Yeah. We had to bribe a-- a lot of the border people which were local people. And one of our guys got hit with a stick in the head, that he got deaf on the spot. One guy. Fortunately, I went through smoothly. And once we crossed the border to the Russian side, they let us go wherever we want to go. So we went to Rowne, like I mentioned. And over there I stayed three, four months, till they arrested me to send to Siberia

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you get-- do you remember how you got from the border to Rowne?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I-- just a horse and buggy. And we knew that-- I knew that they-- they lived there. So I wanted to go to that city, because any other city, I didn't know nobody. So we went to Rowne, and we worked there a little bit, all kind of odd jobs. And it wasn't long enough after that when they came and arrested us.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you still together with this group of people from your town?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Yeah. We were together for a short while. And then they-- we separated. Of course, they-- in Siberia, I was alone in a separate place. I didn't even know where they sent the other guys. But like I understand, he wound up-- one of our friends, one of the friends-- wound up being in the Polish army, but not in the same army that Sikorski, the general, but in a army that-- the Russians formed a Polish army, also, on their own.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he wound up being in that army. And he survived, too. He survived, had a-- he survived in Poland. He married that little-- the girl that went with us, and wasn't a happy marriage. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: And I never heard from them anymore. I saw her once in Israel, the lady. She separated from her husband. She had a son with her, and she lives in Israel, I think, still today.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there-- was there a big Jewish community in Rowne? Was that a--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, there was, before the war, a nice Jewish community. But not much that we had to do with them, because everybody was afraid of their shadow. The Russians weren't angels, either, you know. So they were-- no, everybody was on his own. These People that I lived with happened to be my hometown people, but they were very nice to me. And we tried to get forward, you know, to come and wait and see what the-- what the end is going to be when the war is going to end. And who's going to win, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when you-- when they sent you to-- to Siberia, was this all-- the people, you were all taken and put aboard a train?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were these all Jews? Or were they Poles?

speaker: interviewee
text: A lot of Poles. And mainly Jewish, because the Jews were-- were mostly running away. The Pollacks didn't have to fear there. So they stayed, mostly. But there were a lot of intelligentsia, Polish intelligentsia, which they arrested because they were very much [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] squeezing the Polish intelligentsia. Because they're afraid that this is that element that might hurt them, so they suffered just a lot. I was, as a matter of fact, in Siberia with a man called [INAUDIBLE], which was the head veterinarian doctor of the Polish army. Very big man in the Polish army. He, with his daughter, were there. And they suffered together with us. But how they got away with-- because, of course, once the Polish army formed, he got back his big-- I never saw. Maybe he would have helped me somehow a little, but never saw him anymore. So he-- he was the head veterinarian doctor in the Polish army, because they were having a lot of horses. And that was a very big job, I mean, as a doctor, and as a general in the Polish army.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember that trip? Trip from Rowne to Siberia?

speaker: interviewee
text: Ah, wasn't a pleasant one.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hm.

speaker: interviewee
text: People, you know, they would-- it lasted about four or five weeks. And no facilities. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So people-- the minute the train stopped, woman, man, children went down from, and just made right-- right there at-- at the train. They would-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] physiological necessities they did on the spot' there. The feeding wasn't too bad. I mean, we survived. But it wasn't easy in those car boxes, in those-- to travel to Siberia such a far way. But you survive. You survive. As a matter of fact, there was a woman with two children. And they got separated from her husband, and she was in Siberia. And I tried to help her as much as I could. She was with two little children, she-- but she went to work in the kitchen. And she worked there, and she survived, too. The kids survived. Matter of fact, I met them in Israel after the-- after-- they went out a little later. I don't know how they came to, but they-- they got to be in Israel after the war. '45 or '46, they came to Israel, where I was in '42 already in Palestine.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were these-- were you being-- was this the Russian army that was moving you to Siberia? Or was it the police?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, there were guards. And I suppose they were army, because they were in uniforms with weapons and bayonets and rifles and bayonets. And they were watching, but there weren't too many incidents, because there was nowhere to go, nowhere to run away anymore. Where in-- in that home, you know where to go. But over there, when you are in strange country, and especially already the first three, four days we were away deep in Russia. So I don't exactly remember the route we took, but the destination was Siberia.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened when you got there? Was-- when you finally arrived wherever you were going?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Siberia? When we arrived, we-- they organized labor that you have to work. If you don't work, you don't-- you don't eat. That was the slogan in Russian. [SPEAKING RUSSIAN] So we, being a young man, I decided that I am going to work, and I want to survive. And that's exactly what happened.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were the conditions? Where were you living? What kind of--

speaker: interviewee
text: We're living in barracks. The summer in Siberia is worse than the winter, because there is a little fly-- mosquitoes in the billions, not millions, but billions, literally. And there is a little fly. When we went to work, we wear-- we had a mask over a-- with little holes, very little holes. But that little fly went through that hole and bit you here in the eye. And the eye was swollen. And I had this many times happen to me. About four or five times.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the summer-- and sleeping was impossible in the barracks, because there were lice and mosquitoes. And that was torture.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the summer was worse than the winter. Like, [INAUDIBLE]. In the winter at least, you don't have that, you know, that-- problem. It's cold. It's-- but if you work-- we even took off our jacket because it was too warm to work in the jacket. I came just the way I am-- no extra shoes, no extra jacket, nothing. But they gave me-- they supplied what-- especially the guys that worked hard. After a while, I got a uniform, a warm leather jacket, warm pants, and boots up till here. They called [RUSSIAN], which is what-- they are made out of wool, pressed wool, and very hard. And you wear a rubber boot underneath and on top. And it was fine. It was fine. It was survivable. And we-- we lived in the barracks. Family men got a little better life. They got little homes. Like there was one guy, one fellow, that bought even a cow there. And the grazing was no problem. I don't know what he fed him in the winter, but in the summer, it was no problem, because there was a lot of grass there growing. And he had his own milk. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And it was easier. He was with a wife and a child. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So we went through enough, plenty. But survivorship is a-- is another thing that you fight. You fight for life. So I guess-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and that's exactly what it was. You know, you wanted to live. You want to see your family. You wanted to-- I didn't think of marrying or getting married or what. I just wanted to survive, to survive to see my family.

speaker: interviewee
text: But I never saw my family, anymore, because they were-- they perished in the--

speaker: interviewer
text: Why don't we-- we'll stop right here and change the tape.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.
